asyncVoidMethods.description: Async void methods should be async Task. Async void cannot be awaited and exceptions crash the app.

findings.recommendedFix: Change to 'private async Task LandingPage_LoadedAsync' and call with proper await pattern, or use Loaded += async (s,e) => { await ... };
Change to 'private async Task CoursePage_LoadedAsync' and call with proper await pattern, or use Loaded += async (s,e) => { await ... };
Change to 'private async Task LessonPage_LoadedAsync' and call with proper await pattern
Change to async Task and use proper async event handler pattern, or use async lambda: Loaded += async (s,e) => { await ... };
Use async Task with exception handling wrapper, add try-catch for fire-and-forget safety
Use async Task with exception handling, or keep async void but add try-catch inside
Use async Task with exception handling, or wrap body in try-catch
Use async Task with exception handling wrapper
Use async Task with exception handling, or wrap in try-catch
Use async Task with exception handling
Use async Task with exception handling
Use async Task with exception handling

missingConfigureAwaitFalse.description: Library code should use ConfigureAwait(false) to avoid context capture overhead. In WPF UI code, this is acceptable to omit for UI thread marshaling, but library code should use it.

findings.recommendedFix: In library/service code, add .ConfigureAwait(false) - acceptable here since method returns to UI via Task anyway

missingConfigureAwaitFalse.note: In WPF applications, omitting ConfigureAwait(false) is often intentional to preserve UI thread context. However, pure service/library code should use it.

syncOverAsync.description: Task.Result or Task.Wait can cause deadlocks in UI thread contexts. Use async/await throughout.

findings.recommendedFix: CRITICAL: This is sync-over-async with .Wait() on the UI thread context. Change to 'await enumerator.MoveNextAsync()' throughout
CRITICAL: Use 'await enumerator.DisposeAsync()' instead of .Wait()
N/A - control properly uses async patterns

syncOverAsync.conclusion: Found 2 instances of .Wait() on async operations in LlamaTutorService.WarmUpAsync. These can cause deadlocks in WPF contexts.

fireAndForgetWithoutExceptionHandling.description: Fire-and-forget async calls without exception handling can crash the app or lose errors.

findings.recommendedFix: Add exception handling: _ = CheckRuntimeAsync().ContinueWith(t => Log(t.Exception), TaskContinuationOptions.OnlyOnFaulted)

findings.pattern: await LoadModelAsync() inside async void OnLoaded
findings.recommendedFix: The async void event handler does catch exceptions to the UI, but wrap in try-catch for graceful handling
Fire-and-forget stored in field - exceptions lost. Add .ContinueWith with exception logging

unawaitedAsyncCalls.description: Async calls that aren't awaited can cause race conditions or lost exceptions.

findings.recommendedFix: This is intentional fire-and-forget. Ensure exceptions are handled via ContinueWith or similar
Intentional fire-and-forget with CancellationToken for cleanup. Consider adding exception handling
Intentional fire-and-forget with CancellationToken. Consider adding exception handling

uiThreadBlockingOperations.description: Blocking operations on the UI thread can freeze the application.

findings.recommendedFix: CRITICAL: This blocks threadpool thread. Use await instead
Acceptable - used for UI animation timing, not blocking
Acceptable - UI animation timing

dispatcherInvokeUsage.description: Dispatcher.Invoke vs Dispatcher.InvokeAsync. Invoke is synchronous and can cause deadlocks.

findings.recommendedFix: CORRECT: Using BeginInvoke (async) is proper for WPF UI marshaling
CORRECT: Using BeginInvoke (async) is proper
CORRECT: Using BeginInvoke (async) is proper
LOW RISK: Using Invoke (sync) - consider InvokeAsync for better responsiveness if called from background thread

criticalDeadlockRisks.summary: 2 CRITICAL deadlock risks identified in LlamaTutorService.cs

risks.description: In WarmUpAsync(), the code uses .AsTask().Wait() on IAsyncEnumerator operations within a Task.Run delegate. While Task.Run runs on the threadpool (avoiding classic UI thread deadlock), this is still sync-over-async which can exhaust the threadpool and cause hangs.
risks.impact: Threadpool starvation, potential application hang during warm-up
risks.recommendedFix: Change the entire warm-up logic to be properly async: use 'await foreach' or manually await the MoveNextAsync calls. Consider removing WarmUpAsync entirely or implementing it as a truly async method.

risks.description: Double sync-over-async pattern with DisposeAsync().Wait(). This compounds the blocking risk.
risks.impact: Same threadpool blocking issues as DEADLOCK-001
risks.recommendedFix: Use 'await using' pattern or manually await DisposeAsync()

generalRecommendations: Audit all async void event handlers to ensure they have proper try-catch blocks
Replace all sync-over-async patterns (.Wait(), .Result) with proper await
Consider adding ConfigureAwait(false) to library service methods
Add CancellationToken support to all public async methods
Review fire-and-forget patterns for exception handling
